title: Interplay of Cl Substitution and He$^{+}$ Irradiation in CrSBr$_{1-x}$Cl$_{x}$
authors: Satyam Sahu, Adeel Bukhari, Arijit Kayal, Valerie Černá, Bing Wu, Aljoscha Söll, Gregor Hlawacek, Zdeněk Sofer, Martin Kalbáč, Matěj Velický, Otakar Frank
abstract: Two-dimensional magnetic semiconductors provide a promising platform for exploring the interplay between disorder, lattice dynamics, and resonant light--matter interactions. Among them, CrSBr exhibits strong in-plane anisotropy and pronounced resonance-enhanced Raman scattering. Here, we investigate the effects of Cl substitution and He$^{+}$ irradiation on the vibrational response of CrSBr using polarization-resolved Raman spectroscopy. Cl substitution activates additional phonon modes associated with local symmetry breaking, while He$^{+}$ irradiation introduces distinct defect-related scattering channels and enhanced phonon broadening. The combined effects of alloy disorder and externally introduced defects lead to strong anisotropic reconstruction of the Raman spectra and modification of the nonlinear Raman response under near-resonant 1.96 eV excitation. Power-dependent measurements reveal robust superlinear scaling of both intrinsic and substitution-induced phonon modes, indicating persistent resonance-enhanced electron--phonon coupling even in defect-engineered samples.
summary: This work uses Raman spectroscopy to examine how introducing disorder via chlorine substitution and physical damage via helium ion irradiation affects the vibrational modes of $	ext{CrSBr}_{1-x}	ext{Cl}_x$. The findings show that while disorder significantly alters the spectrum, the underlying strong electron-phonon coupling mechanism persists, indicating the material's resilience in its electronic response.
main_problem: The study investigates how combining compositional disorder (Cl substitution) and external physical damage ($	ext{He}^+$ irradiation) affects the vibrational properties of the layered magnetic semiconductor $	ext{CrSBr}_{1-x}	ext{Cl}_x$.
main_result: The combined disorder leads to anisotropic reconstruction of the Raman spectra, but the resonance-enhanced electron-phonon coupling, evidenced by superlinear power scaling, remains robust even in defect-engineered samples.
method: The research employs polarization-resolved Raman spectroscopy, supplemented by power-dependent measurements to probe the vibrational response.
model_or_system: The physical system is the mixed-halide magnetic semiconductor $	ext{CrSBr}_{1-x}	ext{Cl}_x$, which is analyzed in thin flakes. Disorder is introduced via controlled $	ext{Cl}$ substitution and external $	ext{He}^+$ ion irradiation.
key_observables: Phonon frequencies, linewidths, polarization dependence ($E\parallel a$ vs $E\parallel b$), and the nonlinear scaling exponent ($	heta$) of Raman intensities.
important_parameters: Cl substitution concentration ($x$), $	ext{He}^+$ irradiation fluence (up to $1.2 	imes 10^{15} 	ext{ cm}^{-2}$), and excitation energy (1.96 eV).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the observed spectral changes can be systematically attributed to the distinct effects of alloying disorder versus irradiation-induced defects.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate composition-dependent spectra, fluence-dependent defect mode activation ($	ext{D}1, 	ext{D}3, 	ext{D}\#$), and the power-law scaling of various phonon modes before and after irradiation.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the effects of Cl substitution first, then the effects of He+ irradiation on pure $	ext{CrSBr}$, and finally combines these analyses to study the interplay in $	ext{CrSBr}_{1-x}	ext{Cl}_x$, concluding with nonlinear response measurements.
why_interesting: The study provides detailed experimental evidence on how lattice dynamics are modified by two distinct sources of disorder (chemical vs. physical), which is relevant for understanding material stability and electronic coupling in engineered quantum materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Composition-dependent Raman spectra of bulk CrSBr1−xClx under 2.33 eV excitation for (a) E∥a and (b) E∥b, respectively. Here, the light curves are the raw data, while the darker ones are the fits using a Voigt function.

FIG. 2. Defects in He+ irradiated CrSBr. (a-b) Fluence-dependent Raman spectra of 4L CrSBr, and (c-d) thickness-dependent normalized (w.r.t. Si) Raman spectra at a fluence of 1.2 × 1015 cm−2 excited using a 2.33 eV laser for E∥a (a, c) and E∥b configurations (b, d). The positions of the parent Ag modes and He+ induced D1, D3, and D# modes are highlighted by dotted vertical lines.

FIG. 3. Thickness evolution of D# and D3 modes in CrSBr at a fluence of 1.2 × 1015 cm−2 under 2.33 eV excitation for E∥b configuration. Here, the light curves are the raw data, while the darker ones are the fits using the Voigt function for the peaks highlighted using vertical lines in Figure 2. Inset: ID#/IA3g as a function of number of layers.
FIG. 4. Defects in He+ irradiated CrSBr1−xClx . Thickness-dependent normalized Raman spectra at a fluence of 1.2 × 1015

FIG. 5. Raman intensities as a function of illumination power under resonant (1.96 eV) excitation for E∥a configuration for x = 0.2. (a-b) A2 g, P1, and P2 modes in non-irradiated samples. (c-d) He+ irradiated (2.4 × 1014 cm−2) A2 g and P1,2-modes. Solid circles are the fitted intensities with the bars as the fitting error, while the curves are the fits using I ∝℘θ.
